recipes.title: Chipotle-Style Cilantro Lime Rice
recipes.description: Fresh, zesty, and perfectly fluffy — just like the real thing.

ingredients.name: water or chicken broth (or half and half)

steps.title: Rinse the rice
steps.content: Rinse rice under cold water until the water runs mostly clear. This removes excess starch for fluffier rice.

steps.title: Toast the rice
steps.content: In a saucepan over medium heat, melt butter or oil. Add rice and toast for 2–3 minutes, stirring, until lightly golden and fragrant.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Add water/broth and salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to low. Cover and simmer for 18 minutes without lifting the lid.

steps.title: Steam
steps.content: Remove from heat and let steam, covered, for 5 minutes.

steps.title: Season and serve
steps.content: Fluff with a fork, then stir in lime juice, lime zest, and chopped cilantro. Taste and adjust salt or lime as needed.

recipes.notes: Use half broth, half water for a richer savory flavor. Add cilantro while rice is still warm so it wilts slightly and the flavor blooms.

recipes.title: Crispy Oven-Roasted Garbanzo Beans
recipes.description: Golden, crunchy, and perfectly seasoned — great as a snack, salad topper, or bowl addition.

ingredients.name: cans of garbanzo beans (15 oz each), drained and rinsed

steps.title: Preheat oven
steps.content: Preheat your oven to 400°F (200°C). Line a baking sheet with parchment paper.

steps.title: Dry the beans
steps.content: Spread the drained and rinsed beans on a clean towel. Pat completely dry — moisture is the enemy of crispiness. Let sit 10 minutes if you can.

steps.title: Season
steps.content: Toss the dried beans with olive oil, salt, garlic powder, smoked paprika, cumin, black pepper, and cayenne if using.

steps.title: Roast
steps.content: Spread in a single layer on the baking sheet — don't crowd them. Roast for 25–30 minutes, shaking the pan halfway through, until deeply golden and crispy.

steps.title: Cool and serve
steps.content: Let them cool on the pan for 5–10 minutes — they crisp up further as they cool.

recipes.notes: Drying the beans thoroughly is the #1 key to crispiness — don't skip it. Best eaten within a few hours; they soften overnight.

recipes.title: Smoky Pan-Seared Chicken
recipes.description: Hard-seared, semi-charred crust. Juicy, tender inside. Minimal seasoning — all smoke, all heat.

ingredients.name: neutral oil, high smoke point (avocado or grapeseed) — no butter

steps.title: Dry & pound
steps.content: Pat chicken completely dry with paper towels — this is critical for a hard crust. If breasts are thick (>1 inch), pound to even thickness between plastic wrap so they cook evenly.

steps.title: Season
steps.content: Mix smoked paprika, kosher salt, black pepper, and garlic powder. Coat both sides generously. Let the chicken sit at room temp for 15 minutes — don't skip this.

steps.title: Rip-hot pan
steps.content: Heat your HexClad pan dry over high for 2.5–3 minutes — the hybrid surface needs longer than cast iron to get evenly rip-hot. Then add oil and heat another 30–60 seconds until it just begins to smoke. You want it seriously hot before the chicken goes in.

steps.title: First sear — don't touch
steps.content: Lay chicken away from you into the pan. Press down firmly with a spatula for 10 seconds. DO NOT move it. Let it sear undisturbed until it releases easily and has a deep brown-to-char crust, about 4–5 min.

steps.title: Flip & finish
steps.content: Flip once. Reduce heat to medium-high. Cook until internal temp hits 165°F (74°C), about 3–4 more minutes. Skip butter basting — it'll burn at this heat in a HexClad. Thighs can go slightly higher than 165°F — they benefit from it.

steps.title: Rest
steps.content: Transfer to a cutting board and rest for 5 minutes, loosely tented. Do not cut early — this locks in all the juice.

recipes.notes: HexClad's hybrid surface handles high heat well and gives a solid sear — just preheat longer than you'd expect (2.5–3 min dry before oil). Skip butter basting; it burns at searing temps. You won't get quite the same char depth as cast iron, but the paprika will still do its thing. Use avocado or grapeseed oil only — high smoke point is essential here.
